pairs.question: Who was Edward Cadogan and what were his notable achievements?
pairs.answer: Edward Cadogan (1833–1890) was a British clergyman and rower. He won the Silver Goblets at the Henley Royal Regatta in 1854, partnering with Walter Francis Short. He later became the Rural Dean and Rector of Wicken, Northamptonshire, in 1872.

pairs.question: How did Edward Cadogan contribute to the village school in Wicken?
pairs.answer: Cadogan revitalized the village school in Wicken, increasing enrollment to about 80 children within three years of his arrival. He secured funding through subscriptions and even threatened to establish a school board if donations did not meet expectations. He also offered to transfer management of the school to subscribers or their representatives.

pairs.question: What is known about Edward Cadogan's family lineage?
pairs.answer: Cadogan married Alice Smith in 1856. Their daughter Blanche Ann married Sir William Carlile, MP for Buckingham. Their son, Henry Osbert Samuel Cadogan, served as a Lieutenant-Colonel in the Royal Welch Fusiliers and was killed in action in 1914. A grandson, Edward Henry Cadogan, played first-class cricket for Hampshire, and another grandson, Frank Cadogan Cowper, became a renowned artist.

pairs.question: What significant event marked Edward Cadogan's later years?
pairs.answer: Cadogan died by suicide at his Rectory in Wicken on 16 April 1890. An inquest jury ruled the death as 'suicide whilst temporarily insane'.

pairs.question: What role did Edward Cadogan play at Christ Church, Oxford?
pairs.answer: Cadogan was educated at Christ Church, Oxford, and was also a rower at the university. He partnered with Walter Francis Short to win Silver Goblets at Henley Royal Regatta in 1854.

pairs.question: How much did Edward Cadogan spend on the Rectory in Wicken, and what was the purpose of the expense?
pairs.answer: In 1873, Cadogan added a new wing to the Rectory at Wicken at a cost of £300 shortly after his arrival in the parish.
